meta.title: Triadic Classification of BG Nodes
meta.description: Each of the 123 bg_nodes_v2 nodes classified into tattva (what reality IS), sadhana (what one must DO), or phala (what RESULTS). Based on the proposition field of each node.

bands.tattva: ontological/metaphysical claims about self, brahman, prakriti, gunas, cosmic structure
bands.sadhana: practice, discipline, action, yoga, devotion, renunciation, methods
bands.phala: liberation, states of being, portraits of the realized, fruits, destinations

nodes.rationale: frames the dharmic crisis as an ontological situation — the battlefield as the site of moral tension
vishada as the precondition for teaching — a structural claim about the necessity of crisis
causal claim about what happens when lineage is destroyed — dharmic cosmology
narrative-ontological: war as cosmic event, not personal quarrel
primary claim is about what the wise know (the nature of death); secondarily describes the wise person's state
the core metaphysical claim — atman is unborn, eternal, undying
metaphysical distinction between dehin (self) and deha (body)
prescribes endurance of opposites; grounded in the metaphysical claim that sense-contacts are impermanent
pure ontology — the nature of being (sat) and non-being (asat)
describes the marvellous unknowable nature of the self
prescribes the warrior's duty (svadharma); grounded in the nature of kshatriya-dharma
defines yoga as samatvam — a practice of equanimity in action
prescribes single-pointed resolute intelligence as the method
prescribes transcending the three gunas and Vedic ritualism; grounded in the claim that Vedas deal only with guna-domain
portrait of the sthitaprajna — the realized sage's state of being
causal chain describing how desire leads to ruin — a psychological-ontological claim
the central practice teaching — act without attachment to fruit
ontological claim: no being can exist without action; gunas compel action
cosmic reciprocity of yajna as ontological structure; secondarily prescribes keeping the wheel turning
prescribes acting for the welfare of the world (lokasangraha)
ontological claim: gunas act, not the self; ego falsely claims doership
identifies desire as the enemy — ontological diagnosis; secondarily prescribes overcoming it
ontological hierarchy: senses < mind < buddhi < self
claim about the eternal teaching lineage — paramparic cosmology
metaphysical claim about divine incarnation to restore dharma
result: knowing divine birth liberates from rebirth; grounded in the nature of divine birth
ontological claim: all paths are valid, divine reciprocates in kind
ontological claim: varna is by guna-karma, not birth
paradox of action/inaction — an ontological insight that functions as practice wisdom
knowledge-as-sacrifice: jnana-yajna as the highest practice; grounded in the claim that knowledge burns all karma
prescribes the method of seeking a teacher through prostration, question, and service
ontological diagnosis: doubt destroys; secondarily prescribes cutting doubt with the sword of knowledge
ontological claim: sannyasa and yoga lead to the same end; secondarily endorses karma-yoga as superior
portrait of the non-doer in action — the realized state of one who acts like a lotus leaf on water
describes the result: brahma-nirvana, inner joy, extinction in Brahman
portrait of the equal-visioned sage; grounded in the ontological unity of all beings
ontological claim: Krishna is the enjoyer of all sacrifice, lord of all worlds, friend of all beings
ontological equation: sannyasa = yoga; secondarily prescribes abandoning sankalpa
prescribes self-elevation by the self; grounded in the reflexive self-relationship
portrait of the yogi who sees clod, stone, and gold as equal
detailed meditation method — posture, gaze, diet, practice
prescribes abhyasa and vairagya to restrain the restless mind
result: the fallen yogi is not lost — previous practice carries forward across births
ranks the yogi as supreme above ascetics, scholars, and ritualists
the bhakta-yogi as the highest — a ranking of the realized; secondarily prescribes devotion as the path
vision of self in all beings — a realized state; grounded in the ontological oneness
ontological claim: divine has two natures — para (higher/living) and apara (lower/material)
ontological claim: the divine is the origin and dissolution of all
ontological claim: nothing is higher than the divine; all strung on it like beads
ontological claim: the divine is the essence (taste, light, sound) in all things
ontological claim: three gunas arise from the divine yet the divine transcends them
ontological claim about maya as divine and difficult to cross; secondarily prescribes taking refuge to cross it
typology of four kinds of devotees — a structural classification
ontological claim: Vasudeva is all; secondarily notes this knowledge is rare and liberating
claim about how worship of other deities reaches the divine, though fruits are limited
ontological claim: the divine conceals itself through yoga-maya
ontological diagnosis: all beings are born into duality-delusion from desire and aversion
defines the imperishable Brahman, adhyatma, and karma as cosmic categories
ontological claim: last thought at death determines rebirth; secondarily implies practice of constant remembrance
prescribes uttering OM while dying; the result is attaining the highest goal
describes the result: the great-souled do not return to rebirth
cosmological claim about cyclic time — Brahma's day and night
ontological claim: beyond the unmanifest is another eternal unmanifest
ontological structure of two cosmic paths (light/dark); secondarily describes destinations
meta-claim about the nature of this knowledge: royal, secret, purifying
paradoxical ontological claim: all beings subsist in the divine yet the divine does not dwell in them
cosmological claim: the divine sends forth and reabsorbs all beings through prakriti, seated as indifferent witness
ontological-epistemic claim: the deluded disregard the divine in human form
ontological claim: the divine is father, mother, sustainer, witness, refuge, seed — all cosmic relations
ontological claim about ritual worship: svarga is temporary, merit exhausts; secondarily describes the result of return
prescribes undivided devotion; the result is divine provision and protection
ontological claim: all worship has one true recipient behind all gods
prescribes offering even a leaf with devotion — democratization of worship
prescribes offering all actions to the divine; the result is freedom from karma-bonds
ontological claim: the divine is impartial toward all beings
result: even the worst sinner is saved by devotion; secondarily prescribes undivided devotion
result: all beings regardless of birth can attain the highest goal
cosmogonic claim: seven seers and four Manus born from the divine's mind
ontological claim: all qualities and states arise from the divine alone
ontological claim: the divine is the self in the heart, the beginning-middle-end of all beings
ontological claim: all manifest excellence is a fragment of divine splendor
result: the divine gives liberating wisdom and destroys ignorance for those devoted; secondarily prescribes constant devotion
ontological vision: the entire universe beheld as one in the divine body
prescribes undivided devotion as the only means to see the cosmic form; grounded in epistemology of divine vision
ontological claim: the divine is Time the destroyer; Arjuna is merely the instrument
the devotee's need for an approachable form — prescribes seeking the familiar; describes the terror-comfort dialectic
prescribes saguna bhakti as easier for the embodied — a practical path recommendation
the graduated path: a descending ladder of practices for different capacities
ranking of practices: fruit-renunciation surpasses meditation, which surpasses knowledge-practice
portrait of the ideal devotee — the realized state described through 26 qualities
twenty virtues as prerequisites (conditions) for jnana — a practice list
describes Brahman as the knowable: beginningless, beyond sat/asat, light of lights
Samkhya ontology: purusha and prakriti both beginningless, their roles defined
liberation through the vision of seeing the same lord in all perishable beings; grounded in the ontology of the imperishable within the perishable
cosmogonic claim: Brahman is the womb, the lord places the seed — origin of all beings
ontological claim about how each guna binds the embodied self differently
eschatological destinations determined by dominant guna at death; grounded in guna ontology
portrait of the gunatita — the one who has transcended the three qualities
prescribes bhakti as the vehicle to transcend the gunas; the result is fitness for Brahman
cosmological image of the world as inverted tree; secondarily prescribes cutting it with the axe of non-attachment
ontological claim: the jiva is an eternal fragment of the divine
ontological claim: the lord enters earth, becomes digestive fire, enters the heart as memory and knowledge
the three-purusha ontology: kshara, akshara, and purushottama who transcends both
enumerates the divine qualities (daivi sampad) as equipment for liberation
prescribes abandoning the three gates of hell (kama-krodha-lobha); grounded in their identification as self-destructive
prescribes shastra as the authority for determining right action
ontological-psychological claim: faith is threefold, born of one's nature; a person IS their shraddha
systematic classification and prescription of three types of tapas (body, speech, mind)
prescribes proper giving: right place, time, and recipient — sattvic dana
ontological claim: OM TAT SAT are the threefold designation of Brahman; secondarily prescribes their use in ritual
resolves the sannyasa/tyaga distinction: sattvic tyaga = perform duty, drop the fruit
Samkhya claim: five causes of action; seeing only the self as agent is perverted understanding
classifies knowledge, action, and agent by three gunas — ontological taxonomy; secondarily guides toward sattvic modes
classifies buddhi and dhrti by gunas; secondarily prescribes sattvic buddhi that knows bondage and liberation
classifies pleasure by gunas: sattvic is poison-first-then-nectar; secondarily prescribes choosing sattvic pleasure through practice
ontological claim: varnashrama duties distributed by svabhava; secondarily prescribes devotion to one's own duty
describes the supreme perfection of non-action (naishkarmya-siddhi); secondarily prescribes the path of renunciation
describes the brahma-bhuta state: serene, equal to all, platform for supreme devotion
prescribes fixing the mind on Krishna, surrendering all actions through buddhi-yoga
ontological claim: Ishvara dwells in the heart moving all beings by maya; secondarily prescribes total surrender
the final prescription: abandon all dharmas, take refuge in me alone; result is liberation from all sins
prescribes rules for transmitting the Gita teaching — who should and should not receive it
structural claim: the BG is a reported event within the MBH, witnessed through Sanjaya via Vyasa's grace
